candidate_problems.text: The conjecture assumes constraint propagation is binary (load-bearing vs. decorative), but real explanations may have graded or hierarchical constraint structures — some parts constrain many others, some constrain few. Is there a topology of constraint graphs that would let us rank explanatory elements by their structural centrality, and does this topology predict anything about where explanations fail?

The forcing test (remove a part, observe reorganization) presupposes we can identify 'parts' of an explanation independently of the test itself. But in highly integrated explanations, the decomposition into parts may be theory-laden — we carve the explanation at joints that are themselves products of prior theoretical commitments. Does the constraint-propagation criterion presuppose a solution to the problem of explanatory individuation it was meant to diagnose?

The conjecture claims that when constrained explanations fail, they fail specifically — pointing to the violated assumption. But this assumes the failure mode is detectable and localizable. In complex systems with feedback, a substitution may propagate to contradiction in ways that are computationally intractable to trace. Does high constraint propagation guarantee diagnostic clarity, or only in explanations with certain structural properties (e.g., acyclic constraint graphs)?

Cumulative-case explanations are characterized as lacking a causal skeleton and being non-falsifiable by substitution. But Bayesian confirmation theory treats independent evidence as epistemically virtuous — convergent independent lines of support are harder to dismiss than a single tight chain. Is the conjecture in tension with Bayesian epistemology, or can the two frameworks be reconciled by distinguishing confirmation from explanation?

The conjecture maps onto Deutsch's 'hard to vary' criterion but claims to reveal its structural mechanism. However, 'hard to vary' was originally applied to whole explanations, not to the mutual constraint relations among parts. Does the constraint-propagation account actually explain why good explanations are hard to vary, or does it redescribe the same phenomenon at a finer grain without adding predictive content?

If near-zero internal degrees of freedom is the mark of a good explanation, then a maximally constrained explanation — one where every part is uniquely determined by all the others — would be ideal. But such an explanation would be maximally brittle: any single error anywhere propagates everywhere. Is there an optimal degree of constraint that balances explanatory tightness against error tolerance, and what determines that optimum?

The substitution test requires knowing what counts as a 'contrary detail' — a substitution that should break the explanation if it is load-bearing. But identifying contraries requires background knowledge about what alternatives are live. In domains where the space of alternatives is poorly understood (early-stage science, novel phenomena), the test may be systematically misleading. Does the diagnostic procedure have a domain of applicability condition that the conjecture does not state?

The conjecture treats constraint propagation as a synchronic structural property of a finished explanation. But explanations are constructed and revised over time. Is there a dynamic version of the criterion — one that tracks how constraint structure changes as an explanation develops — and would it predict which research programs are converging on real structure versus accumulating decorative detail?

The conjecture implies that mathematical proofs, which have near-zero internal degrees of freedom by construction, are paradigm cases of load-bearing explanation. But mathematicians distinguish between proofs that explain why a theorem is true and proofs that merely establish that it is true. Does constraint propagation capture explanatory depth in mathematics, or does it conflate logical necessity with explanatory relevance?

If the fragility of a constrained explanation is its epistemic virtue, then adversarial actors could exploit this: constructing explanations that appear tightly constrained but are built around a false load-bearing assumption that is difficult to test. The very property that makes good explanations diagnostically powerful also makes them vulnerable to sophisticated confabulation. Does the criterion need a supplementary condition distinguishing genuine constraint from engineered apparent constraint?

The conjecture applies the substitution test to explanatory parts, but does not address what happens when the *conclusion* is substituted. A load-bearing explanation should also be sensitive to changes in what it is explaining — if the conclusion shifts, the parts should need to shift too. Is bidirectional constraint (parts constrain conclusion, conclusion constrains admissible parts) a stronger or different criterion than the unidirectional version the conjecture describes?

Systems thinking suggests that the same structural property — tight coupling among components — that makes a system explanatorily powerful also makes it prone to catastrophic failure cascades. Is there a direct analogy between load-bearing explanations and tightly coupled systems in Meadows' sense, and if so, does the literature on system resilience offer leverage on when to prefer loose versus tight explanatory structures?
